skill.name: pymatgen
skill.description: Use pymatgen for materials science analysis, including crystal structures, format conversion, phase diagrams, electronic structure data, and Materials Project queries.
skill.summary: Analyze crystal structures, phase diagrams, and materials data with pymatgen.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown examples and scientific API documentation. The confirmed risks are legitimate but elevated: scripts read and write local files, access MP_API_KEY, and query the Materials Project network API, so publication should include a clear warning.

medium_findings.title: Materials Project API Key and Network Access
medium_findings.description: The phase diagram utility reads MP_API_KEY from the environment and uses MPRester to fetch Materials Project entries. This is expected for the skill, but it exposes credential and outbound network behavior that users must understand.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly reads MP_API_KEY and sends authenticated requests to the documented Materials Project API. The behavior is legitimate for this skill and no exfiltration endpoint was found.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Local File Writes
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts write converted structures, exported analysis, and phase diagram images to user-selected paths. This is normal for analysis utilities, but users should run them only in trusted working directories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are directly visible and are driven by command-line output arguments. I found no hidden path or destructive overwrite logic, but filesystem write capability is real.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The large set of external command alerts are shell commands inside documentation and usage examples, not Ruby backtick execution in runnable Ruby code. They mostly show installation commands or how to invoke the included scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced Markdown blocks or inline documentation. No Ruby files or backtick-based shell execution were found in the inspected runnable scripts.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography blocker alerts appear to match ordinary prose, Markdown, package names, or scientific abbreviations rather than cryptographic calls. No evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithms used for security was found in the inspected contexts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines discuss pymatgen architecture, workflow setup, and version notes. They do not perform hashing, encryption, or authentication.

low_findings.title: Dynamic Import Alerts Are Static Import Examples
low_findings.description: The dynamic import alerts point to Python from-import blocks in Markdown examples. These examples import pymatgen modules and do not construct module names from user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged examples are static import statements in fenced documentation. They are not importlib calls, eval calls, or dynamic module loading from untrusted input.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential, Network, and Filesystem Capabilities Combined
dangerous_patterns.description: The analyzer heuristic correctly noticed a combination of credential access, network access, and filesystem writes. Context shows this combination supports Materials Project workflows rather than malicious behavior, but it remains a capability users should review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The combined capabilities are real in the helper scripts, but the endpoints and data flow match the documented materials-science use case. I found no evidence of covert exfiltration or prompt injection.

content.value_statement: Materials researchers need reliable workflows for crystal structures, phase diagrams, and computational data. This skill provides pymatgen guidance, scripts, and references for common materials science tasks.
actual_capabilities: Guide crystal structure creation, reading, writing, and manipulation with pymatgen core classes.
Convert structure files among CIF, POSCAR, XYZ, JSON, YAML, and other supported formats.
Analyze symmetry, lattice parameters, density, coordination environments, and distance matrices.
Generate phase diagrams and stability summaries using Materials Project entries.
Reference workflows for VASP input sets, transformations, slabs, interfaces, band structures, and DOS.
limitations: Materials Project queries require a valid MP_API_KEY and network access.
Scientific results depend on input file quality and installed pymatgen dependencies.
The scripts do not sandbox file paths or restrict output locations.
The skill provides examples and utilities, not independent validation of published materials data.

use_cases.title: Prepare Crystal Structures
use_cases.description: Convert CIF, POSCAR, and related structure files before running simulations or sharing datasets.

use_cases.title: Assess Phase Stability
use_cases.description: Build phase diagrams from Materials Project data and estimate energy above hull for candidate compounds.

use_cases.title: Teach Pymatgen Workflows
use_cases.description: Use focused examples to explain structure objects, symmetry analysis, input sets, and transformation workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Structure File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pymatgen skill to explain how I can read a CIF file and report formula, density, and space group.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Structure Formats
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pymatgen skill to convert my POSCAR workflow into CIF and JSON outputs, including command examples and checks.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Phase Diagram
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pymatgen skill to create a Li-Fe-O phase diagram workflow and explain how to analyze LiFeO2 stability.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Screening Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pymatgen skill to design a screening workflow with transformations, VASP input sets, and cached Materials Project data.

output_examples.input: Analyze structure.cif with symmetry and coordination details.
output_examples.output: A concise report listing formula, lattice constants, density, space group, site table, and nearest-neighbor coordination notes.

output_examples.input: Convert several CIF files into POSCAR format for VASP.
output_examples.output: A batch conversion plan with the expected command, output directory, validation checks, and common format pitfalls.

output_examples.input: Evaluate whether LiFeO2 is stable in the Li-Fe-O system.
output_examples.output: A phase-diagram workflow that fetches entries, builds the convex hull, reports stability, and describes likely decomposition products.

best_practices: Use explicit input and output paths, and review generated files before using them in simulations.
Store Materials Project API keys in environment variables, not in prompts or shared files.
Validate structures for symmetry, atom overlap, and reasonable lattice parameters before analysis.
anti_patterns: Do not paste private API keys into chat messages or notebooks.
Do not run batch conversions in directories containing unrelated files.
Do not treat generated phase diagrams as publication-ready without checking data provenance and convergence.

faq.question: Does this skill require pymatgen to be installed?
faq.answer: Yes. The scripts and examples assume pymatgen is installed in the active Python environment.

faq.question: Can it access Materials Project data?
faq.answer: Yes, but only when mp-api is installed and MP_API_KEY is available in the environment.

faq.question: Can it convert CIF and POSCAR files?
faq.answer: Yes. The conversion script and examples use pymatgen structure readers and writers for common formats.

faq.question: Does it run external simulation software?
faq.answer: No. It can generate inputs for tools such as VASP, but it does not launch those simulations.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with proprietary structures?
faq.answer: Use care. Local structure files stay local unless you run Materials Project queries or share data in prompts.

faq.question: What should I check before trusting results?
faq.answer: Check input validity, dependency versions, API data sources, convergence assumptions, and unit conventions.
